source_title: Enhanced synaptic plasticity in newly generated granule cells of the adult hippocampus
node_relevance_reason: Primary electrophysiology paper for dg_immature_granule_neuron: demonstrates that young adult-born granule cells have distinct active and passive membrane properties compared to mature granule cells, including T-type Ca2+ channels enabling Ca2+ spikes, and a lower threshold for LTP induction — directly filling the electrophysiology gap in the dg_immature_granule_neuron and dg_mature_granule_neuron nodes.
summary: Schmidt-Hieber et al. 2004 show in adult mouse hippocampus that newly generated granule cells have substantially different membrane properties from mature cells, express T-type Ca2+ channels capable of generating isolated Ca2+ spikes and amplifying Na+ action potentials, and exhibit a lower threshold for associative LTP induction. These unique mechanisms are proposed to facilitate new memory formation by providing immature neurons with enhanced synaptic plasticity. No neuropeptide data are reported. Species: mouse.
quotes: young granule cells in the adult hippocampus have substantially different active and passive membrane properties from mature cells
Young neurons express T-type Ca2+ channels, which are able to generate isolated calcium spikes and to amplify fast sodium action potentials
Associative long-term potentiation can be induced more easily in young neurons than in mature neurons under identical conditions
newly generated neurons express unique mechanisms to facilitate synaptic plasticity, which may be important for the formation of new memories

source_title: Neuronal differentiation in the adult hippocampus recapitulates embryonic development
node_relevance_reason: Provides comprehensive patch-clamp characterisation of the full maturation sequence from silent neuron through GABA-only to GABA+glutamate stages in adult mouse DG, with precise dpi milestones and membrane property values (Vrest, Rin, Cm) at each stage — directly filling the electrophysiology gap for dg_neuroblast and dg_immature_granule_neuron nodes. Documents depolarised EGABA of GABAergic inputs in immature neurons.
summary: Espósito et al. 2005 used retroviral GFP labelling in adult female C57BL/6 mice to track newly born dentate granule cells through whole-cell patch-clamp recordings. Neurons progress through a stereotyped sequence: silent (1–7 dpi, high Rin 4.3 GΩ, low Cm 6.4 pF, Vrest −50.6 mV) → slow GABAergic inputs only (8–18 dpi, Vrest −45.6 mV) → glutamatergic inputs added (≥18 dpi, Vrest −63.2 mV, Cm 24.6 pF) → fast perisomatic GABAergic inputs last. GABAergic PSCs show a depolarised reversal potential (EGABA = −26.9 mV), indicating immature chloride homeostasis. This sequence mirrors embryonic hippocampal development, suggesting cell-autonomous programming. No neuropeptides reported.
quotes: All GFP+ neurons studied between 1 and 7 dpi were silent, and their morphology seemed to correspond to class A or B.
Neurons with GABAergic but not glutamatergic afferents were first observed at 8 dpi.
Glutamatergic PSCs were detected in GFP+ neurons bearing GABAergic inputs from 18 dpi onwards.
The development of afferent synaptic connections takes ∼4 weeks and follows the sequence: silent → slow GABA → glu → fast GABA.

During the first week neurons had an irregular shape and immature spikes and were synaptically silent. Slow GABAergic synaptic inputs first appeared during the second week...In contrast, glutamatergic afferents were detected by the fourth week in neurons displaying mature excitability and morphology. Interestingly, fast GABAergic responses were the latest to appear.
GABAergic-evoked PSCs displayed a reversal potential (EGABA) of -26.9 ± 1.9 mV.
Silent neurons: Vrest (mV): − 50.6 ± 2.3; Cm (pF): 6.4 ± 0.4; Rinput (MΩ): 4.3 ± 0.5
GABA neurons: Vrest (mV): − 45.6 ± 2.3; Cm (pF): 11.7 ± 1.2; Rinput (MΩ): 2.9 ± 0.4
GABA and glu neurons: Vrest (mV): − 63.2 ± 2.8; Cm (pF): 24.6 ± 1.4; Rinput (MΩ): 0.62 ± 0.10
It is striking that the sequence of events observed in the adult DG is also followed during hippocampal development.

The finding that the developmental sequence for neuronal maturation in the developing and adult brain is preserved in environments that are so distinct suggests that such sequence is primarily determined by a cell-autonomous program rather than by environmental cues.

source_title: Neuronal differentiation in the adult hippocampus recapitulates embryonic development
node_relevance_reason: Characterises GABA synaptic current kinetics at the immature granule neuron stage: slow dendritic currents precede fast perisomatic currents, with distinct EGABA values for each component, demonstrating a somatic-to-dendritic chloride gradient. Critical for filling the dg_immature_granule_neuron electrophysiology gap regarding GABAergic input maturation.
summary: Espósito et al. 2005 describes two GABA PSC components in immature adult-born dentate granule cells: a slow dendritic component (EGABA −44.5 ± 2.1 mV) and a fast perisomatic component (EGABA −30.9 ± 1.6 mV). The depolarised EGABA of both components reflects elevated intracellular Cl− in immature neurons. The fast perisomatic component is selectively blocked by somatic BMI application, while the slow dendritic component requires bath application. Fast GABAergic perisomatic inputs are the final synaptic inputs acquired during maturation. Species: adult female C57BL/6 mouse.
quotes: Current-voltage curves revealed two components with similar conductance (Gmax): an early current with a depolarized EGABA (-30.9 ± 1.6 mV; n = 9) and fast kinetics and a late current with a more hyperpolarized EGABA and slow kinetics (-44.5 ± 2.1 mV; n = 12).
This difference in EGABA for the fast and slow components is consistent with a gradient for the intracellular Cl- concentration between the somatic and dendritic compartments.
The kinetics of these GABAA-mediated PSCs is reminiscent of slow dendritic responses rather than fast perisomatic currents described in hippocampal neurons.
Spontaneous PSCs displayed a low frequency (0.05 ± 0.02 Hz; n = 6) and long rise and decay times.

source_title: GABA regulates synaptic integration of newly generated neurons in the adult brain
node_relevance_reason: Establishes the tonic and phasic GABA activation sequence in adult-born dentate granule cells with precise dpi chronology: tonic GABA precedes any phasic input (from 3 dpi), GABAergic PSCs first at 7 dpi, glutamatergic PSCs first at 14 dpi. Documents NKCC1-dependent high [Cl−]i as the mechanism for GABA depolarisation. Directly addresses the key electrophysiology gap in dg_neuroblast and dg_immature_granule_neuron nodes regarding GABA excitation.
summary: Ge et al. 2006 demonstrate in adult mice (C57Bl/6, 7-8 weeks) using whole-cell and gramicidin perforated patch-clamp recordings that newborn dentate granule cells receive tonic GABA activation from 3 dpi before any detectable synaptic input, followed by phasic GABAergic PSCs at 7 dpi and glutamatergic PSCs at 14 dpi. Newborn neurons express high NKCC1 and little KCC2, resulting in high intracellular [Cl−] and a depolarised EGABA that renders GABA excitatory rather than inhibitory. Knockdown of NKCC1 shifts EGABA negative, and NKCC1-knockdown neurons show significant defects in dendritic arborisation and synapse formation, demonstrating that GABA depolarisation is required for normal synaptic integration. No neuropeptides are reported.
quotes: At 3 days post viral injection (3 dpi), none of the GFP+ cells recorded under voltage-clamp (Vm= −65 mV) exhibited any spontaneous synaptic currents
tonic current in all GFP+ DGCs recorded from 3 dpi and onwards (n = 48)
Bicuculline (10 μM)-sensitive GABAergic PSCs...and CNQX (50 μM)-sensitive glutamatergic PSCs...were first detected in some GFP+ DGCs at 7 dpi and 14 dpi, respectively
newborn DGCs (DCX+) in the adult brain express high levels of NKCC1 and little KCC2
newborn neurons in the adult brain...follow a stereotypical integration process-receiving tonic GABA activation first, followed by GABAergic synaptic inputs and finally glutamatergic synaptic inputs
GABA exerts a depolarising action during the initial development of new DGCs
tonic GABA activation depolarises newborn DGCs...and constitutes the majority of GABA-induced activation
shRNA-NKCC1+ DGCs exhibited significant defects in their dendritic arborisation
The total dendritic length and branch number of these new neurons...were significantly reduced.

source_title: GABAergic excitation promotes neuronal differentiation in adult hippocampal progenitor cells
node_relevance_reason: Documents GABAergic depolarisation of type-2 neuronal progenitor cells (dg_neuroblast equivalent) in the SGZ using GFP-targeted patch-clamp recordings in nestin-GFP mice — directly addressing the electrophysiology gap for the dg_neuroblast node. Shows that GABA input to progenitors drives [Ca2+]i increase and NeuroD expression, linking electrical activity to neuronal fate commitment.
summary: Tozuka et al. 2005 used GFP-targeted recordings from type-2 neuronal progenitor cells in hippocampal slices from nestin-GFP mice and found that these cells receive direct GABAergic (not glutamatergic) neural inputs from the hippocampal circuitry. GABA depolarises type-2 progenitors due to their elevated [Cl−]i. This depolarisation initiates a [Ca2+]i increase and NeuroD expression. BrdU pulse labelling with GABAA-R agonists confirms that GABAergic excitation promotes activity-dependent neuronal differentiation. No neuropeptides are reported. Species: mouse (nestin-GFP transgenic).
quotes: neuronal progenitor (type-2) cells receive active direct neural inputs from the hippocampal circuitry
This input was GABAergic but not glutamatergic.
The GABAergic inputs depolarized type-2 cells because of their elevated [Cl(-)](i).
This excitation initiated an increase of [Ca(2+)](i) and the expression of NeuroD.
GABAergic inputs to hippocampal progenitor cells promote activity-dependent neuronal differentiation.

source_title: Functional neurogenesis in the adult hippocampus
node_relevance_reason: Landmark paper providing the first direct patch-clamp evidence that newly generated neurons in the adult mouse hippocampus fire action potentials, have functional synaptic inputs, and are electrophysiologically integrated by 4 weeks post-infection — directly filling the dg_immature_granule_neuron electrophysiology gap. Documents passive and active membrane properties of adult-born vs mature granule cells.
summary: van Praag et al. 2002 used retroviral GFP labelling in adult female C57Bl/6 mice and whole-cell patch-clamp recordings (4–8 weeks post-injection) to show that newly generated granule cells fire action potentials (Vth −45.8 mV, plateau ~140 Hz), have smaller membrane capacitance (42.3 pF vs 99.2 pF in mature cells) and shorter time constants (16.6 ms vs 33.7 ms), and receive spontaneous synaptic inputs (0.4–5 Hz) including perforant path-evoked responses. Resting membrane potential is slightly depolarised compared to mature cells (−69.7 vs −74.8 mV). These properties confirm functional neuronal integration into the hippocampal circuit by 4 weeks. No neuropeptides are reported.
quotes: newly generated cells in the adult mouse hippocampus have neuronal morphology and can display passive membrane properties, action potentials and functional synaptic inputs similar to those found in mature dentate granule cells
Action potentials displayed a threshold (Vth) of −43 mV, conspicuous after hyperpolarization and frequency adaptation
membrane potentials recorded in response to depolarizing current steps displayed repetitive spiking with increasing frequency, reaching a plateau at about 140 Hz
Membrane capacitance (pF): Newly generated 42.3 (10.2); Mature 99.2 (12.7); P=0.003
Time constant (ms): Newly generated 16.6 (4.0); Mature 33.7 (3.7); P=0.003
Resting potential (mV): Newly generated −69.7 (1.7); Mature −74.8 (1.1); P=0.06
Spiking threshold (mV): Newly generated −45.8 (2.9); Mature −39.6 (1.5); P=0.07
newly generated granule cells are functionally integrated into the circuitry by 4 weeks
Experiments on newly generated neurons (GFP+) were carried out 4–8 weeks after viral injection.
